title: NVIDIA Vera Rubin vs Blackwell Comparison — What Actually Changed
text:
I was halfway through a Blackwell cluster capacity plan when Jensen Huang walked on stage at GTC 2026 and casually announced that my carefully budgeted rack config was already last-gen. That was March 17th. By March 18th, my boss had forwarded me three articles with the subject line “should we wait?” and no other text. So I spent the next few days doing what any reasonable infrastructure engineer would do: obsessively reading spec sheets, cross-referencing leaked benchmarks, and building a nvidia vera rubin vs blackwell comparison spreadsheet that I’m still not fully confident in.
Some of what follows is solid data. Some of it is NVIDIA marketing math I’m still trying to verify. I’ll flag which is which.
Last updated: March 21, 2026. Based on NVIDIA’s GTC 2026 keynote (March 17) and published spec sheets.
What NVIDIA Actually Announced at GTC 2026
The Vera Rubin platform isn’t a single chip. It’s six purpose-built chips designed to work together in a rack-scale system. The headline piece is the Rubin GPU, but calling it just a GPU undersells what’s happening. The full platform:
- Rubin GPU — 336 billion transistors, dual-reticle die, HBM4
- Vera CPU — 88 custom Olympus cores (Arm v9.2), 176 threads
- NVLink 6 Switch — 400G SerDes, 28.8 Tbps switching bandwidth
- ConnectX-9 SuperNIC — PCIe 6.0, 48-lane, full RDMA
- BlueField-4 DPU
- Spectrum-6 Ethernet Switch
The rack config, the Vera Rubin NVL72, packs 72 Rubin GPUs and 36 Vera CPUs into a single liquid-cooled unit. NVIDIA claims 3.6 exaflops of FP4 inference compute, 20.7 TB of HBM4, and 54 TB of LPDDR5X.
The NVIDIA Vera Rubin vs Blackwell Comparison That Actually Matters
I’ve seen a lot of articles quoting NVIDIA’s “5x inference” and “10x cost per token” claims like they’re peer-reviewed benchmarks. They’re not. They’re projections from NVIDIA’s marketing team. I’m not saying they’re wrong, but I’ve been burned by vendor benchmark claims before. Remember when the H100 was supposed to be “30x faster than A100” and the real-world number on my transformer workload was closer to 4x? Yeah.
Anyway, here’s the side-by-side based on what NVIDIA has officially published:
| Spec | Blackwell GB200 NVL72 | Vera Rubin NVL72 | 
|---|---|---|
| GPU Transistors | 208B | 336B | 
| GPU Memory (per GPU) | 192 GB HBM3e | 288 GB HBM4 | 
| Total System HBM | 13.5 TB | 20.7 TB | 
| Memory BW (per GPU) | 8 TB/s | ~13 TB/s (HBM4, estimated) | 
| NVFP4 Inference (per GPU) | 5–10 PFLOPS (varies by sparsity) | 50 PFLOPS (NVIDIA claim) | 
| NVLink BW (per GPU) | 1.8 TB/s (NVLink 5) | 3.6 TB/s (NVLink 6) | 
| CPU | Grace (72 Arm Neoverse cores) | Vera (88 Olympus cores, Arm v9.2) | 
| CPU-GPU Coherent BW | 900 GB/s (NVLink-C2C gen 1) | 1.8 TB/s (NVLink-C2C gen 2) | 
| PCIe | Gen 5 | Gen 6 | 
| GPU TDP | 1.2 kW (B200) / 1.4 kW (B300) | 1.8–2.3 kW | 
| Rack Power | ~120 kW | ~350 kW | 
| Estimated Rack Price | ~$2.8–3.2M | ~$3.5–4M (analyst est.) | 
| Availability | Shipping now | H2 2026 | 
A few things jump out. The memory bandwidth improvement is real but maybe not as dramatic as the headline “5x inference” suggests. You’re going from 8 TB/s to roughly 13 TB/s per GPU. About 1.6x. The inference improvement comes from a combination of the bandwidth gains, the massively increased transistor count, and new FP4 compute units. Not purely bandwidth-driven.
The Blackwell FP4 per-GPU figure deserves a caveat. NVIDIA’s published numbers vary depending on dense vs sparse operations and which model architecture you’re running. I’ve seen figures from about 5 PFLOPS to 10 PFLOPS depending on the source. The Vera Rubin 50 PFLOPS claim is presumably the best-case scenario too, so the real-world multiplier between generations is probably somewhere between 3x and 8x, not a clean 5x.
The Power and Cooling Situation Is Where This Gets Real
I need to spend serious time on this because it’s the part most nvidia vera rubin vs blackwell comparison articles gloss over, and it’s the part that will actually determine whether your org can deploy Vera Rubin at all.
The TDP Numbers Keep Moving
The Rubin GPU’s TDP has been a moving target. NVIDIA originally spec’d it at 1.8 kW per chip. Then reports surfaced that they’d bumped it to 2.3 kW, possibly to maximize competitive performance against AMD’s MI400 series, though the exact motivation is anyone’s guess. Power target changes happen for a dozen reasons and NVIDIA isn’t explaining this one. Regardless, that’s almost double the B200’s 1.2 kW. Multiply by 72 GPUs in an NVL72 rack and you’re looking at somewhere between 130 kW and 350+ kW per rack, depending on which power number turns out to be accurate and how much the networking and CPU subsystems add.
The Vera Rubin NVL72 uses 45-degree hot water liquid cooling. Not optional. You cannot air-cool this thing. If your facility doesn’t have a liquid cooling loop, you’re not deploying Vera Rubin. Period.
My Attempt to Pin Down Actual Rack Power
I spent about an hour trying to figure out the exact rack power envelope and got three different numbers from three different sources. Tom’s Hardware says one thing, SemiAnalysis says another, and NVIDIA’s own spec sheet is frustratingly vague. My best estimate is around 200–350 kW per rack for the standard NVL72, with Rubin Ultra (coming 2027) pushing toward 600 kW in the new Kyber rack form factor.
This is kind of a tangent, but it reminds me of a project I worked on two years ago where we provisioned a row of Blackwell racks and underestimated the cooling requirements by about 15%. Had to emergency-install supplemental CDUs three weeks after deployment. Three weeks. My facilities team still brings it up. The power specs on paper never quite match the power draw under sustained mixed workloads. I expect Vera Rubin to be even more unpredictable in real-world thermals because the HBM4 stacks are denser and the TDP headroom is narrower.
If you’re doing capacity planning right now and you haven’t called your colocation provider about power delivery upgrades, start there. Not with the GPU specs. The specs don’t matter if you can’t plug the thing in.
The Vera CPU Is Probably the Sleeper Hit
Everyone’s focused on the Rubin GPU, and fair enough. But the Vera CPU is where NVIDIA quietly made a massive architectural bet.
Grace was NVIDIA’s first datacenter CPU. Solid, if unremarkable. 72 Arm Neoverse V2 cores, decent memory bandwidth, but it always felt like a side dish. Vera is different. NVIDIA designed the Olympus cores from scratch: custom microarchitecture on Arm v9.2, 88 cores, 10-wide fetch/decode, 2-way SMT for 176 threads, and a claimed 1.5x IPC improvement over Grace. Memory subsystem supports up to 1.5 TB of LPDDR5X at 1.2 TB/s.
The important part: NVLink-C2C gen 2 doubles the coherent CPU-GPU bandwidth to 1.8 TB/s. For agentic AI workloads where the CPU handles orchestration, tool-calling, and sandbox execution, that coherence is critical. NVIDIA claims 50% faster agentic sandbox performance, and this is one claim I actually find plausible based on the architectural changes.
Should You Wait for Vera Rubin or Buy Blackwell Now?
This is the question I keep getting from my boss, our VP of engineering, and a friend at a mid-size AI startup who has a PO ready for a Blackwell cluster.
My honest answer: it depends on your timeline and what you’re running.
Buy Blackwell now if:
- You need compute in production before Q4 2026
- Your facility can’t handle 350 kW racks or doesn’t have liquid cooling
- Your workloads are well-optimized for CUDA on current-gen and you don’t want early-adoption driver issues
- Your budget is allocated and you lose it if you don’t spend it (enterprise reality)
Wait for Vera Rubin if:
- You’re specifically planning for large MoE model training (the 4x fewer GPUs claim could save serious money at scale)
- You’re building an agentic AI platform where CPU-GPU coherence matters
- Your data center build-out timeline already extends into H2 2026 or later
- You care about inference cost per token more than raw training throughput
The pricing gap is smaller than you’d think. Analyst estimates (Futurum Group has been cited, though I haven’t verified their exact methodology) put Vera Rubin racks at roughly $3.5–4M, about 25% more than Blackwell. But if the 5x inference performance claim holds even partially, say 3x in real workloads, the cost per useful FLOP drops dramatically. The ROI math could flip in Vera Rubin’s favor within months of deployment. Could. I haven’t run those numbers with real electricity costs for your specific region, and that matters more than people realize.
The same TCO-vs-sticker-price trap I wrote about in Best AI Agent Evaluation Tools for Developers — I Tested Five and My Traces Were a Mess shows up here too. The rack price is maybe a third of what you’ll actually spend over three years once you factor in power, cooling, networking, and the engineering hours to migrate workloads.
The Software Stack Question
Every hardware generation brings a new round of “will my CUDA code just work?” anxiety. And every time, the answer is “mostly, but not entirely.”
NVIDIA has been good about backward compatibility, but Vera Rubin introduces new compute types (extended FP4 support and new in-network compute on NVLink 6 switches) that existing kernels won’t automatically exploit. If you want that 5x inference claim, you’re going to need updated libraries, recompiled models, and possibly architectural changes to your serving pipeline.
I haven’t seen the actual CUDA toolkit version that ships with Rubin support. NVIDIA’s developer blog mentions it’ll be part of the “standard CUDA ecosystem,” but “standard” is doing a lot of heavy lifting in that sentence. In my experience, day-one driver support for a new architecture means about three months of random segfaults and obscure NCCL errors before things stabilize. I could be wrong, maybe they’ve gotten better at this. But I’ve been doing this long enough to be skeptical.
Oh, and one more thing: the NVLink 6 switches reportedly have several TFLOPS of FP8 in-network compute. NVIDIA claims around 14 TFLOPS, though I haven’t found independent verification of that number. That’s compute happening inside the network switches, not on the GPUs. It’s designed for collective operations, MoE routing, and synchronization. But it means your distributed training code might need to be aware of a third compute domain: GPU, CPU, and now network. NVIDIA is downplaying that complexity in the marketing materials.
What the Cloud Providers Are Doing
AWS, Google Cloud, Azure, Oracle, CoreWeave, Lambda, Nebius, and Nscale are all listed as launch partners. NVIDIA’s official announcement also names Anthropic, Meta, OpenAI, and xAI, which tells you where the first racks are actually going: the big model training labs, not your startup.
For the rest of us who rent GPU time, the realistic timeline is probably Q1 2027 before Vera Rubin instances are widely available at competitive spot pricing. Blackwell instances will likely get cheaper as providers shift inventory. That’s the play I’d personally make at a startup: grab discounted Blackwell capacity in late 2026 while everyone chases Rubin allocations. I wrote about evaluating tooling options in Snyk vs SonarQube Developer Security Comparison — I Ran Both on the Same Codebase and the Results Disagreed, and the same principle applies when your entire GPU fleet might change in 12 months.
Feynman Is Already on the Roadmap
Because NVIDIA never lets you feel comfortable with a purchase, Jensen also confirmed the next architecture: Feynman, with a CPU called Rosa (after Rosalind Franklin). No real specs. I’m not going to speculate because I have exactly zero data points, and anyone telling you what Feynman will deliver is making stuff up.
Frequently Asked Questions
When does NVIDIA Vera Rubin actually ship?
NVIDIA says Vera Rubin is in full production and ships to partners in H2 2026. Cloud provider instances on AWS, Azure, GCP, and others will likely follow in late 2026 or early 2027, depending on allocation priority. The big AI labs (OpenAI, Anthropic, Meta) get first dibs.
Will existing CUDA code run on Vera Rubin without changes?
Basic CUDA code should maintain backward compatibility. But to get the performance gains NVIDIA is marketing, you’ll need updated libraries, new CUDA toolkit versions, and potentially reworked kernels for FP4 compute and in-network compute features. Expect 2–3 months of rough edges after launch based on how previous architecture transitions have gone.
My Actual Take After Several Days of Reading Spec Sheets
So yeah. After watching the keynote three times (once live, once at 1.5x to catch details, once more to screenshot the spec slides), here’s where I’ve landed on the nvidia vera rubin vs blackwell comparison:
Vera Rubin is a legitimate generational leap, not an incremental refresh. The HBM4 move, the custom Olympus CPU cores, the doubled NVLink bandwidth, the in-network compute: these are real architectural changes, not just more transistors on the same design. The 5x inference claim is probably optimistic for real workloads, but even 2.5–3x would be significant.
But the power requirements are brutal. If your org is evaluating this as a Blackwell replacement, don’t just compare GPU specs. Compare your facility’s power delivery, cooling infrastructure, and the timeline to upgrade both. That’s where most deployments will actually bottleneck.
My spreadsheet still has question marks in about a third of the cells. Some specs NVIDIA hasn’t confirmed. Some I’ve seen conflicting numbers for. And none of this includes real-world benchmarks, because Vera Rubin isn’t shipping until H2 2026. I’ll update this comparison when independent benchmarks start coming in, probably late Q3 or early Q4 if the timeline holds.
For now, I’m telling my team to proceed with our Blackwell order. We need the compute in June. But I’ve flagged the power upgrade in our 2027 facility plan. Because if even half of NVIDIA’s claims pan out, Vera Rubin is what we’ll be running in 18 months.
And I’ll probably have to redo this entire capacity plan from scratch. Again.
